title: Boards & Matrices
summary: The everyday project boards and matrices, in scan-friendly blocks: the Kanban board for flow, the RACI/RAM for who does what, the power-interest grid for stakeholder engagement, and the probability-impact matrix for risk. Framed against PMBOK 8 principles and performance domains, with tailoring and value cues throughout.

sections.heading: Why these visuals matter
sections.body: Boards and matrices are artifacts you tailor to context, not mandatory paperwork. Each one supports a performance domain and honours a principle: choose the lightest visual that lets the team see the work and deliver value.

rows.value: Governance + Scope. Makes flow visible; supports Build an Empowered Culture.
Resources. Clarifies roles; supports Be an Accountable Leader and accountability.
Stakeholders. Targets engagement effort where it changes outcomes.
Risk. Prioritises risk responses; supports Adopt a Holistic View.

blocks.title: Tailoring reminder
lines: Artifacts are chosen, not mandated.
Simple project -> a whiteboard column list; complex -> a fuller board or matrix.
The visual exists to drive a decision, not to be filed.
Be suspicious of an answer that says 'always produce the full matrix'.

sections.heading: Kanban board
sections.body: A visual board that pulls work through columns - typically To Do, In Progress, and Done. A work-in-progress (WIP) limit caps how many items a column may hold, which exposes bottlenecks and keeps the team focused on finishing before starting.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 330 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='58' y='20' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>To Do</text><text x='165' y='20' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>In Progress</text><text x='165' y='33' fill='#B5462F' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>WIP limit 2</text><text x='272' y='20' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>Done</text><rect x='10' y='40' width='96' height='150' rx='5' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.2'/><rect x='117' y='40' width='96' height='150' rx='5' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.3'/><rect x='224' y='40' width='96' height='150' rx='5' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.2'/><rect x='18' y='50' width='80' height='24' rx='3' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='18' y='80' width='80' height='24' rx='3' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='18' y='110' width='80' height='24' rx='3' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='125' y='50' width='80' height='24' rx='3' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.3'/><rect x='125' y='80' width='80' height='24' rx='3' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.3'/><rect x='232' y='50' width='80' height='24' rx='3' fill='#E7F1ED' stroke='#2F6F5E' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='232' y='80' width='80' height='24' rx='3' fill='#E7F1ED' stroke='#2F6F5E' stroke-width='1'/></svg>
figure.caption: Cards pulled left to right; the middle column is capped at its WIP limit.

items.title: Visualise the work
lines: Every item is a card in a column.
Columns model your real workflow stages.

items.title: Limit WIP
lines: Cap each column's card count.
Lower the limit to surface bottlenecks.

items.title: Pull, don't push
lines: Pull a new card only when capacity frees up.
Finish before starting.

items.title: Manage flow
lines: Watch where cards pile up.
Improve the slowest stage first.

rows.value: To Do -> In Progress -> Done (mirror your workflow).
The key lever; a capped column exposes the constraint.
New work starts only when downstream frees up.
Lead time, cycle time and throughput; a cumulative flow diagram shows trends.

blocks.title: Exam tip
lines: Kanban = visualise flow, limit WIP, pull work.
A WIP limit is the primary lever - lower it to surface bottlenecks.
Reduce bottlenecks by finishing work, not by starting more.

sections.heading: RACI (responsibility assignment matrix)
sections.body: A grid that maps activities (rows) to roles (columns), recording each role's involvement. RACI is the most common form of the responsibility assignment matrix (RAM).

font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>C</text><text x='165' y='162' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>R responsible · A accountable · C consulted · I informed</text></svg>
figure.caption: Roles across the top, activities down the side; one A per row.

items.text: Responsible - does the work. One or more per activity.
Accountable - owns the outcome. Exactly one per activity.
Informed - kept in the loop; one-way communication.

groups.label: Charter
lines: PM: R
Sponsor: A
Team: C
QA: I

groups.label: Plan
lines: PM: A
Sponsor: C
Team: R
QA: I

groups.label: Build
lines: PM: A
Sponsor: I
Team: R
QA: C

rows.value: Activities or deliverables.
Roles or people.
Single point of accountability; it cannot be shared.
No owner - a gap to fix.
One role is R on too much - a bottleneck.

blocks.title: Exam tip
lines: Exactly one 'A' per row - one person is accountable.
Several can be R, C or I, but accountability can't be shared.
RACI is a form of RAM; a RAM can also roll up at a summary level.

sections.heading: Power-interest grid
sections.body: A 2x2 that plots stakeholders by their power (ability to affect the project) against their interest (how much they care). Each quadrant implies a different engagement strategy - the point is to spend engagement effort where it changes the outcome.

groups.label: High interest
lines: Manage closely.
Partner and collaborate; these drive success.

groups.label: Low interest
lines: Keep satisfied.
Don't overload them, but don't lose them.

groups.label: High interest
lines: Keep informed.
Useful supporters; consult on detail.

groups.label: Low interest
lines: Monitor.
Minimal effort until their position shifts.

rows.value: Authority vs concern (the default grid).
Authority vs active involvement.
Involvement vs ability to change the project.
Power, urgency and legitimacy combined.

blocks.title: Exam tip
lines: High power + high interest = manage closely.
High power + low interest = keep satisfied.
The grid feeds the engagement plan; the stakeholder register still says who they are.
Re-plot as positions change - engagement is continuous, not one-off.

sections.heading: Probability-impact matrix
sections.body: A grid that scores each risk by probability (how likely) against impact (how bad or good), giving a risk score that sets priority. It drives qualitative risk analysis - which risks get a response first.

groups.label: Red - high
lines: High probability and/or high impact.
Respond first; assign an owner now.

groups.label: Yellow - medium
lines: Moderate score.
Watch and plan a response.

groups.label: Green - low
lines: Low probability and impact.
Accept or add to a watch list.

blocks.title: Scoring
blocks.note: Same math sizes threats and opportunities

lines: Higher score = higher priority.
Rank all risks, then respond top-down.
Expected monetary value.
Sum identified risks to size contingency reserve.

rows.value: Escalate, Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, Accept.
Escalate, Exploit, Share, Enhance, Accept.

blocks.title: Exam tip
lines: Probability x Impact sets qualitative priority.
The matrix ranks risks; it does not by itself resolve them.
Both threats and opportunities live on the matrix - optimise responses per Adopt a Holistic View.
New risk -> register it, analyse P and I, then plan the response and assign an owner.

sections.heading: Quick distinctions
sections.body: The traps these visuals get tested on, side by side.

rows.value: Kanban shows flow of work; RACI shows ownership of work.
RACI is one form of RAM; RAM is the general responsibility grid.
Stakeholder register = who they are; power-interest grid = how to engage.
Matrix = risk priority; risk register = the full list and responses.

blocks.title: Final tip
lines: Match the visual to the performance domain the question is really about.
Pick the tailored, value-delivering option over the heaviest process.
Boards and matrices inform decisions - the exam rewards acting on them.

source: PM Cram study notes - reworked into structured blocks and aligned to PMBOK 8 principles and performance domains.
